b1.title: Block 1
b1.sub: M5 review + metrics by business goal

cards.body: A media plan follows an order: first you choose the objective, the buying type, the budget, the audience, the placements and the creative. All of that decides how your ad is delivered, but it doesn't tell you whether it worked. Measuring is the last step: that's where you verify whether the campaign actually moved the needle for the business. Without measurement, you just spent money blindly. That's why this module closes the loop: you learn to read whether what you built was worth it. Before we dive in, let's do a quick review of what you saw in Module 5.

cards.q: The placements default recommended by Meta is ___.
cards.explanation: Advantage+ placements: more surfaces, more optimization.

cards.q: The creative affects auction cost because:
options: It influences estimated action rates and ad quality (total value)
cards.explanation: It moves 2 of the 3 components of total value. That's why the creative is a cost lever.

cards.q: Advantage+ ___ adapts cuts, music and text per person.
cards.explanation: Advantage+ creative optimizes the creative per person.

cards.q: Restricting placements without a business reason:
options: Raises the cost and reduces delivery
cards.explanation: Fewer auctions/combinations = higher cost. A reminder from Module 5.

lessons.title: Learn · Interpreting metrics by business goal

cards.body: There is no universal 'best metric' that works to judge any campaign. The metric that matters depends on the business goal, that is, what you actually wanted to achieve. If you were trying to get more people to know your brand, you care about recall (how many people remember seeing the ad). If you were after sales, you care about cost per result. If you were after revenue, you care about ROAS. The mental rule is simple: first define what you wanted to achieve and then choose the metric that proves that achievement, never the other way around.

cards.h: Example: a coffee shop opening a new location
cards.body: Imagine "Café Aurora", a coffee shop that opens a second location and launches a campaign so the neighborhood knows it exists. Its business goal is to get known, not to sell online. If the owner judges the campaign by how many online sales it generated, they're measuring something they weren't aiming for and will believe it failed. The right metric for them is how many people it reached and how many remember the ad. Same money, a completely different read depending on the metric you choose.

Vanity metrics are numbers that look good but don't prove the business moved forward: lots of likes, lots of impressions or lots of loose clicks. The problem is you can have thousands of likes and zero sales, or a ton of clicks that never turn into customers. The serious planner reports the metric that answers the business goal and leaves vanity metrics as context, not as the verdict. They boost the ego, not the business.

cards.h: Example: a viral post with no business
cards.body: "Tienda Lumo", an online clothing store, had a video with 200 thousand likes and got excited. But on review, that video didn't generate a single sale because it attracted people who only wanted the joke, not to buy. The 200 thousand likes are a vanity metric: they impress in the meeting but don't make payroll. The metric that really mattered was how many purchases and at what cost. That's why the planner never confuses popularity with a business result.

rows: Reach, frequency, estimated ad recall lift
Link clicks, landing page views, CPC
Results, cost per result, conversion rate

cards.label: REPORTING RULE
cards.body: Before opening Ads Manager, define the metric that proves the business goal. Report everything else as context, not as the verdict.

cards.label: TRAP
cards.body: Judging an Awareness campaign by CPC or by purchases is a measurement error: that objective doesn't optimize for clicks or sales. It's measured by recall and efficient reach.

cards.q: An Awareness campaign for a rebrand reports a high CPC. The client wants to 'fix it'. What do you answer?
options: Switch to Traffic to lower the CPC
CPC is not the Awareness metric; it's evaluated by reach and estimated ad recall lift
Raise the bid to improve the CPC
cards.explanation: Awareness doesn't optimize for clicks; CPC is irrelevant for that objective. The right measurement is reach, frequency and estimated ad recall lift. Switching to Traffic would abandon the business goal.

cards.q: Is there a universal 'best metric' to judge any campaign?
options: No, the relevant metric depends on the business goal
cards.explanation: There is no universal metric. The relevant metric depends on the business goal: recall for Awareness, cost per result for conversion, ROAS for revenue.

cards.q: For an Awareness business goal, the key metrics are reach, frequency and estimated ad recall ___.
cards.explanation: Awareness is measured by reach, frequency and estimated ad recall lift.

cards.q: Which key metric corresponds to a Revenue business goal?
cards.explanation: The Revenue goal is measured with purchase value and ROAS. Clicks/CPC belong to Traffic; reach/recall belong to Awareness.

cards.q: For a Traffic goal, the key metrics are link clicks, landing page views and ___.
cards.explanation: Traffic is measured by link clicks, landing page views and CPC.

cards.q: For a Leads / Sales goal, what are the key metrics?
options: Results, cost per result, conversion rate
cards.explanation: Leads / Sales is measured by results, cost per result and conversion rate.

cards.label: CASE TO SOLVE
cards.body: A furniture store in Monterrey did a rebrand and launched a campaign with the Awareness objective. A week later, the owner calls you upset: "The report says the CPC is super expensive and there are almost no purchases. This campaign is a disaster." You have to set the measurement conversation straight.

cards.q: What's the first point you clarify for the owner?
options: That CPC and purchases are the right metrics to judge Awareness
That Awareness doesn't optimize for clicks or sales; it's evaluated by reach, frequency and estimated ad recall lift
That the campaign should be paused immediately
That they should raise the bid to lower the CPC
cards.explanation: Awareness doesn't optimize for clicks or sales: judging it by CPC or purchases is a measurement error. It's evaluated by reach, frequency and estimated ad recall lift.

cards.q: The owner says: 'Then switch it to Traffic so the CPC goes down.' What do you answer?
options: Good idea, that way the report looks better
Switching to Traffic would abandon the business goal of building brand awareness after the rebrand
The objective doesn't matter, all metrics are interchangeable
Only if you first raise the frequency
cards.explanation: The business goal is to build brand awareness (rebrand). Switching to Traffic just to 'improve' the CPC abandons the real objective. The metric must follow the objective, not the other way around.

cards.q: Which metrics should this rebrand Awareness campaign actually be judged by?
options: Reach, frequency and estimated ad recall lift
Cost per result and conversion rate
cards.explanation: Awareness is measured by reach, frequency and estimated ad recall lift, not by CPC, conversion rate or ROAS.

cards.q: Is there a universal 'best metric'?
cards.a: No. The relevant metric depends on the business goal.

cards.q: How is Awareness measured?
cards.a: Reach, frequency and estimated ad recall lift (not CPC or sales).

cards.q: How is Traffic measured?
cards.a: Link clicks, landing page views and CPC.

cards.q: How are Leads / Sales measured?
cards.a: Results, cost per result and conversion rate.

cards.q: How is the Revenue goal measured?
cards.a: Purchase value and ROAS.

cards.q: Typical measurement trap
cards.a: Judging Awareness by CPC or by purchases: that objective doesn't optimize for clicks or sales.

cards.h: Did you cause the result or was it going to happen anyway?
cards.body: Incrementality answers the most honest question in advertising: did those sales happen because of your ad, or were they going to happen anyway? It measures the causal effect of the campaign, that is, how many conversions would NOT have occurred if you had never run ads. It's different from counting all the sales and crediting them to the ad, because a lot of people were already going to buy. To measure it for real you need an experiment: you compare a group that did see ads against a group that saw nothing. That comparison is the only way to isolate the campaign's real contribution.

A holdout group (control group) is a set of people who, on purpose, do NOT see your ads during the experiment. It works like a "parallel universe" without advertising. If the group that did see ads converts more than the holdout, that difference is the real effect of your campaign. If they convert almost the same, it means your ads contributed little and those sales were going to happen anyway. Without a holdout there's no point of comparison, and without comparison you can't prove causality.

cards.h: Example: a sneaker brand measuring its real contribution
cards.body: "RunFlex", a sports sneaker brand, sold 1,000 pairs the month it ran ads and celebrated. But it ran an experiment: it didn't show ads to a holdout group, and that group, on its own, still generated the equivalent of 600 sales (repeat customers and brand searches). The real effect of the campaign was the 400 extra sales, not the 1,000. Without the holdout, RunFlex would have overestimated its advertising by 2.5 times. That's exactly what incrementality measures.

rows: Compares two variants (creative, audience, strategy) with separate audiences
Measures incremental conversions with a control group (holdout)
Measures changes in recall, awareness or intent via survey
Group that does NOT see ads; the basis for comparing incrementality

cards.label: ATTRIBUTION ≠ INCREMENTALITY
cards.body: Attribution assigns credit to touchpoints; incrementality asks what would have happened without the ad. To prove the real value of the investment, the gold standard is the lift experiment.

cards.label: TRAP
cards.body: Using last-click attribution as 'proof' that the ads generated the sales. Attribution doesn't answer causality: for that you need a lift experiment with a holdout.

cards.q: The client doubts whether the reported sales would have happened the same without ads. What methodology do you propose?
options: Conversion Lift with a holdout group
cards.explanation: The question is causal (incrementality). Conversion Lift compares exposed vs. holdout to isolate the conversions the campaign actually generated. Attribution doesn't answer causality.

cards.q: What does incrementality measure?
options: The credit assigned to each touchpoint
The causal effect of the ads: conversions that would NOT have happened without ads
cards.explanation: Incrementality measures the causal effect of advertising: how many conversions would NOT have happened without the ads.

cards.q: The group that does NOT see ads and serves as the basis for comparing incrementality is called the ___.
cards.explanation: The holdout is the control group that does NOT see ads; it's the basis for comparing incrementality.

cards.q: Which experiment measures changes in recall, awareness or intent via survey?
cards.explanation: Brand Lift measures changes in recall, awareness or intent via survey (exposed vs. control).

cards.q: The experiment that compares two variants (creative, audience, strategy) with separate audiences is the ___ Test.
cards.explanation: The A/B Test compares two variants with separate audiences.

cards.q: How does attribution differ from incrementality?
options: Attribution assigns credit to touchpoints; incrementality asks what would have happened without the ad
Attribution measures recall; incrementality measures CPC
Attribution is a lift experiment; incrementality isn't
cards.explanation: Attribution assigns credit to touchpoints; incrementality asks what would have happened without the ad. To prove real value, the gold standard is the lift experiment.

cards.label: CASE TO SOLVE
cards.h: B2B software company in Guadalajara doubts the value of the ads
cards.body: A B2B software company in Guadalajara invests heavily in Meta. The marketing director says: "The sales the report attributes to Meta probably would have come in through our sales team anyway. How do you prove to me that the ads really generate extra sales?" Design the measurement response.

cards.q: What kind of question is the director asking?
cards.explanation: He's asking whether the sales would have happened the same without ads: that's incrementality (causal effect), not attribution.

cards.q: Which Meta experiment directly answers that doubt?
options: Conversion Lift with a holdout group
Raise the frequency and compare weeks
cards.explanation: Conversion Lift compares exposed vs. holdout to isolate the conversions the campaign actually generated. Attribution doesn't answer causality.

cards.q: Additionally, they want to know whether the brand campaign moved the recall and purchase intent of the B2B audience. Which experiment do you add?
cards.explanation: Brand Lift measures changes in recall, awareness or intent via survey between exposed and control groups. Conversion Lift is for conversions; A/B Test compares variants; ROAS Goal is a bid strategy.

cards.q: Incrementality
cards.a: Causal effect of the ads: conversions that would NOT have happened without ads.

cards.q: Conversion Lift
cards.a: Experiment with a holdout group that measures incremental conversions.

cards.q: Brand Lift
cards.a: Measures changes in recall/awareness/intent via survey (exposed vs. control).

cards.q: A/B Test
cards.a: Compares two variants (creative, audience, strategy) with separate audiences.

cards.q: Holdout
cards.a: Group that does NOT see ads; the basis for comparing incrementality.

cards.q: Attribution vs incrementality
cards.a: Attribution = credit per touchpoint. Incrementality = what happened as a result of the ads.

cards.body: To measure well, Meta recommends not marrying a single method but using three lenses that complement each other. The first is experiments, which prove real causality with test and control groups. The second is attribution, which distributes the credit of each sale across the touchpoints (each time the person saw or interacted with your brand). The third is marketing mix modeling (MMM), which looks at the aggregate contribution of ALL channels, even offline ones. Each lens has blind spots, so they're used together.

cards.h: Each lens answers a different question
cards.body: The key is to understand which question each one answers. Experiments answer "did my ads cause this result?". Attribution answers "which touchpoints were involved on the path to the conversion?". MMM answers "how do all my channels, online and offline, contribute to sales at a macro level?". None of them alone gives the full picture: the experiment proves cause but is expensive and one-off, attribution is daily but doesn't prove cause, and MMM sees the forest but not the tree. That's why combining them gives the most reliable read.

cards.h: Example: a gym chain and its three questions
cards.body: "VitalFit", a gym chain, uses all three lenses at once. It runs an experiment to prove that its Meta ads really generate new sign-ups (causality). It uses daily attribution to see which ads and formats touched the people who signed up (day-to-day optimization). And once a year it commissions an MMM to understand how Meta compares to its printed flyers, its local radio and its in-store sales team. Three different questions, three different tools, one single strategy.

rows: Prove the causality of a tactic or channel (gold standard)
Daily tactical optimization; see which touchpoints are involved
Macro view: how all channels (on/offline) contribute to sales
Combine all three for a robust read

cards.label: ATTRIBUTION SETTING
cards.body: The attribution window (e.g. 7-day click, 1-day view) defines which conversions are credited to Meta. Changing it changes the reported number without changing the real sales: communicate this to the client.

cards.label: TRAP
cards.body: Treating attribution as the sole proof of causality. It's for tactical optimization; causality is proven by lift experiments.

cards.q: The CMO wants to understand how ALL channels (TV, search, social, offline) contribute to annual sales. Which methodology?
cards.explanation: MMM models the aggregate impact of all channels (online and offline) at a macro level. Attribution only sees measurable digital touchpoints; Brand Lift and A/B Test answer narrower questions.

cards.q: What are the three measurement approaches Meta describes as complementary?
options: Experiments, attribution and marketing mix modeling (MMM)
cards.explanation: Meta describes experiments (causality), attribution (credit per touchpoint) and MMM (aggregate impact of all channels).

cards.q: The approach that gives the macro view of how all on/offline channels contribute to sales is ___.
cards.explanation: Marketing Mix Modeling (MMM) gives the macro view of how all on/offline channels contribute to sales.

cards.q: What is the gold standard for proving the causality of a tactic or channel?
cards.explanation: Experiments are the gold standard for proving the causality of a tactic or channel.

cards.q: Combining experiments + attribution + MMM for a robust read is called ___.
cards.explanation: Triangulation = combining experiments + attribution + MMM for a robust read.

cards.q: What does the attribution setting (e.g. 7-day click, 1-day view) do?
options: Defines which conversions are credited to Meta; changing it changes the reported number, not the real sales
cards.explanation: The attribution window defines which conversions are credited to Meta. Changing it changes the reported number without changing the real sales.

cards.label: CASE TO SOLVE
cards.h: B2B client with a large budget demands proof
cards.body: A B2B client with a large budget demands proof that the Meta investment generates real sales, not just sales that would have happened anyway. You have to build the measurement plan you'll defend in front of their leadership.

cards.q: What measurement plan do you propose as the core?
options: Only the Ads Manager attribution report
Conversion Lift (incrementality) as the core, supported by attribution for daily optimization
cards.explanation: The demand is causal: Conversion Lift proves incremental sales. Attribution serves daily tactical optimization, but doesn't prove causality on its own.

cards.q: The client then wants a macro view of how Meta fits alongside TV, search and their offline sales force. Which layer do you add?
cards.explanation: MMM models the aggregate impact of all on/offline channels at a macro level. It's the right layer for that global view.

cards.q: How do you describe attribution's role in this plan?
options: As the sole proof of causality
As a complementary layer for daily tactical optimization, not as the sole proof of causality
As a replacement for Conversion Lift
cards.explanation: Measure by the business goal, prove real value with lift experiments and use attribution + MMM as complementary layers, not as the sole proof of causality.

cards.q: Marketing Mix Modeling (MMM)
cards.a: Models the aggregate impact of all channels (on/offline) at a macro level.

cards.q: Gold standard for proving causal value
cards.a: Lift experiments (holdout).

cards.q: What does the attribution setting do?
cards.a: Defines the window (e.g. 7-day click/1-day view) that decides which conversions are credited to Meta.

cards.q: Measurement triangulation
cards.a: Combining experiments + attribution + MMM for a robust read.

cards.q: Attribution's role in the plan
cards.a: Daily tactical optimization; a complementary layer, not the sole proof of causality.

cards.q: Module wrap-up
cards.a: Measure by the business goal, prove value with lift experiments, use attribution + MMM as complementary layers.

lessons.title: Integrative case · Measurement plan for Kenet Solar

cards.label: INTEGRATIVE CASE
cards.h: Kenet Solar wants to defend the investment to leadership
cards.body: Kenet Solar runs Awareness campaigns (regional rebrand) and Sales campaigns (quote leads). Leadership demands three things: (1) to know whether the rebrand moved recall, (2) to prove the ads generate sales that wouldn't have happened, and (3) to understand how Meta fits alongside its offline sales force.

cards.q: To judge the rebrand Awareness campaign, which metrics do you report?
options: Reach, frequency and estimated ad recall lift
cards.explanation: Awareness is measured by reach, frequency and estimated ad recall lift; not by CPC or sales.

cards.q: To find out whether the rebrand moved recall, which experiment do you run?
cards.explanation: Brand Lift measures changes in recall/awareness/intent via survey between exposed and control groups.

cards.q: To prove the ads generate incremental sales (not the ones that would have happened anyway), what do you propose?
options: Conversion Lift with a holdout group
cards.explanation: The question is causal: Conversion Lift compares exposed vs. holdout. Attribution doesn't prove causality.

cards.q: To see how Meta fits alongside the offline sales force at an annual level, which layer do you add?
cards.explanation: MMM models the aggregate impact of all on/offline channels at a macro level.

lessons.title: Match the key concepts of Module 6
lessons.instruction: Tap the term and then its definition. Each mistake costs 1 ❤️.
pairs: Reach, frequency, estimated ad recall lift
Link clicks, landing page views, CPC
Results, cost per result, conversion rate
Causal effect: conversions that wouldn't have happened
Group that does NOT see ads; the comparison basis
Compares two variants with separate audiences
Aggregate impact of all on/offline channels

lessons.title: Order the measurement plan
lessons.instruction: This is how the planner builds a campaign's measurement. Reorder with ▲▼ and verify.
items: Define the metric that proves the business goal
Report that metric as the verdict (the rest, context)
Design a lift experiment to prove causal value
Use attribution for daily tactical optimization
Add MMM for the multichannel macro view
lessons.explanation: The metric follows the objective; causal value is proven with experiments; attribution and MMM are complementary layers, not the sole proof.

cards.q: Awareness is measured by reach, frequency and estimated ad recall ___.
cards.explanation: Estimated ad recall lift, not CPC or sales.

cards.q: The Revenue goal is measured with purchase value and ___.
cards.explanation: Purchase value and ROAS.

cards.q: ___ measures the causal effect of the ads.
cards.explanation: Incrementality = conversions that wouldn't have happened without ads.

cards.q: The group that does NOT see ads is called the ___.
cards.explanation: Holdout: the basis for comparing incrementality.

cards.q: The incremental-conversions experiment with a holdout is ___ Lift.
cards.explanation: Conversion Lift.

cards.q: The aggregate contribution of all on/offline channels is given by ___.
cards.explanation: Marketing Mix Modeling.

cards.q: Changing the attribution setting changes the reported number, not the real ___.
cards.explanation: It only changes which conversions are credited to Meta.

cards.q: An Awareness campaign reports a high CPC. How is it correctly evaluated?
options: By reach, frequency and estimated ad recall lift
cards.explanation: Awareness doesn't optimize for clicks or sales.

cards.q: Is there a universal 'best metric'?
options: No, it depends on the business goal
cards.explanation: The metric follows the objective.

cards.q: The Revenue goal is measured with:
cards.explanation: Purchase value and ROAS.

cards.q: What does incrementality measure?
options: Causal effect: conversions that wouldn't have happened without ads
cards.explanation: Causal effect of the ads.

cards.q: The control group that does NOT see ads:
cards.explanation: Holdout.

cards.q: Conversion Lift is used to:
options: Measure incremental conversions with a holdout
cards.explanation: Incrementality of conversions.

cards.q: Brand Lift measures:
cards.explanation: Recall/awareness/intent (survey).

cards.q: A/B Test:
options: Compares two variants with separate audiences
cards.explanation: Two variants, separate audiences.

cards.q: Attribution vs incrementality:
options: Attribution = credit per touchpoint; incrementality = causality
cards.explanation: Attribution doesn't prove causality.

cards.q: Macro view of all on/offline channels:
cards.explanation: Marketing Mix Modeling.

cards.q: Gold standard for proving causality:
cards.explanation: Experiments with a holdout.

cards.q: Changing the attribution setting:
options: Changes the reported number, not the real sales
cards.explanation: It only changes which conversions are credited to Meta.

questions.q: Is there a universal metric for every campaign?
options: No, it depends on the goal

questions.explanation: Conversions that wouldn't have happened without ads.
It's the control group that does NOT see ads.
Conversion Lift measures conversions; Brand Lift measures recall.

questions.q: Does changing the attribution setting change the real sales?
questions.explanation: It only changes the reported number.

cards.q: Is there a universal 'best metric'?
cards.a: No. The relevant metric depends on the business goal.

cards.q: How is Awareness measured?
cards.a: Reach, frequency and estimated ad recall lift (not CPC or sales).

cards.q: How is Traffic measured?
cards.a: Link clicks, landing page views and CPC.

cards.q: How are Leads / Sales measured?
cards.a: Results, cost per result and conversion rate.

cards.q: How is the Revenue goal measured?
cards.a: Purchase value and ROAS.

cards.q: Typical measurement trap
cards.a: Judging Awareness by CPC or by purchases.

cards.q: Incrementality
cards.a: Causal effect of the ads: conversions that would NOT have happened without ads.

cards.q: Holdout
cards.a: Group that does NOT see ads; the basis for comparing incrementality.

cards.q: Conversion Lift
cards.a: Experiment with a holdout group that measures incremental conversions.

cards.q: Brand Lift
cards.a: Measures changes in recall/awareness/intent via survey (exposed vs. control).

cards.q: A/B Test
cards.a: Compares two variants (creative, audience, strategy) with separate audiences.

cards.q: Attribution vs incrementality
cards.a: Attribution = credit per touchpoint. Incrementality = what happened as a result of the ads.

cards.q: Marketing Mix Modeling (MMM)
cards.a: Models the aggregate impact of all channels (on/offline) at a macro level.

cards.q: Gold standard for proving causal value
cards.a: Lift experiments (holdout).

cards.q: What does the attribution setting do?
cards.a: Defines the window (e.g. 7-day click/1-day view) that decides which conversions are credited to Meta.

cards.q: Measurement triangulation
cards.a: Combining experiments + attribution + MMM for a robust read.

cards.q: Attribution's role in the plan
cards.a: Daily tactical optimization; a complementary layer, not the sole proof of causality.

cards.q: Module wrap-up
cards.a: Measure by the business goal, prove value with lift experiments, use attribution + MMM as complementary layers.

cards.q: Vanity metrics
cards.a: Likes/impressions/clicks that look good but don't prove the business goal.

cards.q: Does attribution prove causality?
cards.a: No; for causality, lift experiments with a holdout.

cards.q: Conversion Lift vs Brand Lift
cards.a: Conversion = incremental conversions. Brand = recall/intent via survey.

cards.q: When MMM?
cards.a: Multichannel on/offline macro view at an aggregate/annual level.
